description: The pronoun 'thia' is Phi's second-person singular: the one addressed, English you. It works unchanged as subject or object and can also possess a noun or follow a preposition. Plural address is 'lo thia'; politeness, when wanted, belongs to the separate particle 'pi' (POL).
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip lightly between the upper and lower teeth for unvoiced 'th'. Air passes along it into high-front unrounded 'i'. Keep 'i' and the following open-central 'a' in separate syllables, with stress on 'thi'.
sound_symbolism: The tongue shows briefly between the teeth for 'th', then the two vowels open the mouth. In Phi, that visible beginning can recall the plainness of direct address.
usage_notes: A bare 'thia' that opens a predicate clause means you as subject. A following noun becomes possessive only when the larger sentence rules out subjecthood; predicative possession uses 'phelu' (hold). The unquantified plural is 'lo thia'.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The pronoun identifies the addressee, while separate 'pi' marks a polite manner. The same you works across formal and familiar relationships.
